content.text: No Array (Run Every Render)
When you call
useEffect
with only a callback and no second argument, React runs the effect after
every

completed render — the initial mount and each subsequent re-render. This is the least-constrained form of the hook and is rarely what you want in production code, but understanding it is essential for grasping why the dependency array exists in the first place.

attrs.alt: No dependency array: effect fires after every render

content.text: How It Works

React re-runs the effect because without a dependency array it cannot know whether the effect's work is still valid. It takes the conservative approach: run it every time to guarantee correctness. The effect callback is called after paint, and any cleanup from the previous run is called first.
Cleanup Fires Before Every Re-Run

Because the effect re-runs after every render, the cleanup also runs before every re-run. This means on each render cycle: (1) the previous cleanup fires, then (2) the new effect fires. This is guaranteed — there is never a render where the old effect is alive and the new one has also fired.
The guaranteed cleanup-before-rerun order means you can write effects that are self-consistent: the new run always starts fresh from whatever state the cleanup left the external system in.
The Infinite Loop Trap
The most dangerous pitfall: calling
setState
inside a no-array effect. The state update triggers a re-render, which triggers the effect again, which calls
setState
again — an infinite loop that freezes the browser tab.
Rule:
never call
setState

inside a no-array effect unless you are absolutely certain the state update will not trigger another render that meets the effect's condition. In practice, always add a dependency array when the effect updates state.
Legitimate Use Cases
Synchronising to an External System Every Render
Despite the risks, there are legitimate uses for the no-array form. The key constraint is that the effect must
not
trigger a state update (which would cause a re-render) and must be cheap enough to run on every render without degrading performance.
DOM Property Sync Without State Feedback

Direct DOM property updates that do not trigger re-renders are another valid use. Writing to a ref's DOM node, or updating a measurement library, can happen safely on every render because it does not feed back into React's state.

Using no array here is a valid tradeoff: the dependency array version is more efficient but requires correctly listing every value used inside the effect. The no-array version always stays correct at the cost of one extra execution per unrelated re-render.
Development Debugging

During development, the no-array form is convenient for logging the current value of every prop and state variable after each render. Because you are only logging (a side effect with no state update), there is no feedback loop.
Performance Cost and When to Add a Dependency Array

Every invocation of a no-array effect has a cost: calling the cleanup, calling the effect, and any work inside both. For expensive operations (network requests, heavy computation, large DOM traversals), always add a dependency array to avoid redundant work on unrelated re-renders.
Effect work
Cost per run
Recommendation
console.log
Negligible
No array acceptable in dev
DOM property write
Low
No array acceptable if deps are hard to enumerate
Canvas redraw
Medium
Prefer [dep1, dep2] to skip unchanged renders
Fetch / network request
High
Always use dependency array
setInterval / WebSocket
High (resource allocation)
Always use dependency array
Heavy computation (sort, filter large array)
High
Move to useMemo, not useEffect
No Array vs useLayoutEffect Without Array
If the no-array sync work involves DOM measurement or must happen before paint, prefer
useLayoutEffect
without a dependency array. Both run every render, but layout effects run synchronously before paint while passive effects run asynchronously after paint.
Migrating to a Dependency Array

If you start with a no-array effect and later add dependencies, the effect becomes reactive. This is a safe migration: you are making the effect more precise about when it runs, not changing what it does.

When adding a dependency array to an existing no-array effect, run the ESLint exhaustive-deps rule to catch every value you are using inside the callback. Missing a dependency is the most common source of stale closure bugs.
Checking If an Effect Runs on Every Render
Add a render counter or
useRef
to verify how many times an effect fires without a dependency array:
The Stale-Closure-Free Guarantee

One genuine advantage of the no-array form is that it is always stale-closure-free. Because the effect runs after every render, it always captures the freshest values of every variable it closes over. There is never a stale-closure problem — the tradeoff is that you pay the cost of every run.
This property makes the no-array form useful during development when you are still figuring out exactly which values an effect depends on. Once identified, declare them in the array for efficiency.
Relationship to componentDidUpdate
In the class component model,
componentDidUpdate
(without any equality checks) was the closest equivalent to a no-array
useEffect
— it also ran after every update. But
componentDidUpdate
did not run on mount (that was
componentDidMount
). The no-array
useEffect
unifies both: it runs on mount and every update.
The hooks version is shorter and does not need the guard pattern (
if (prevProps.x !== this.props.x)
) because you add a dependency array when you want conditional execution instead of adding if-checks inside the effect body.
Strict Mode Interaction
In development with React Strict Mode, a no-array effect runs
more

often than in production, because Strict Mode double-mounts components. You will see the pattern: effect → cleanup → effect on mount, followed by cleanup → effect → cleanup → effect on each re-render. The count doubles in development.
Behaviour with Concurrent Features

In React's concurrent renderer, renders can be interrupted and abandoned. However, effects only run after a render has committed — abandoned renders never trigger effects. So even with no dependency array, the effect fires exactly once per committed render.
When using
startTransition

, deferred renders may be interrupted many times before committing. A no-array effect tied to that render still fires only once — after the eventual commit. The no-array property is about committed renders, not render attempts.
Identifying Unnecessary No-Array Effects with the Profiler

When profiling React applications, a component whose no-array effects run frequently shows up in the React DevTools Profiler as a component with many passive-effect invocations. This is a signal to add a dependency array and skip redundant runs.
The No-Array Form as a Developmental Starting Point
When integrating a new external system, it is sometimes helpful to start with no dependency array to confirm the effect works correctly on every render before narrowing it down:
Write the effect with no array to confirm it behaves correctly.
Run the app and observe the effect firing on every render via console logs.
Run ESLint exhaustive-deps to get a list of required dependencies.
Add the dependency array with those values.
Re-test to confirm the effect fires at the correct times and not more often.

This staged approach avoids shipping a no-array effect to production while still using it as a development scaffold. The ESLint plugin makes stage 2 mechanical — run it once and it tells you what to add.
Summary
A
useEffect
with no dependency array runs after every render. React has no information about what the effect depends on, so it conservatively re-runs it every time the component renders.
No second argument = effect fires after every render (mount + all re-renders).
Cleanup from the previous run fires before the next effect run on every re-render.
Never call setState inside a no-array effect — it creates an infinite render loop.
Correct uses: sync DOM writes, external system updates, development logging.
Incorrect uses: fetching data, subscriptions, timers, any expensive per-render work.
For efficiency, add a dependency array as soon as you know what the effect depends on.
The no-array form is a superset of [] and [dep] — either can replace it when deps are declared.
